role_summary: Co-founder (with Huey P. Newton) of the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense in Oakland, October 1966; the party's first chairman; the eighth defendant of the Chicago Eight trial (severed to the Chicago Seven after the gag-and-restraint ordeal in Judge Hoffman's courtroom, 1969).

photo.alt: Bobby Seale, from the Black Panther newspaper, October 5, 1968
photo.license: Public domain (U.S., published 1968 without copyright notice)

ai_reading: Seale is named in the bios of Aaron Dixon (entry 1), Elbert Howard (entry 35), Elmer Dixon (entry 37), and the catalog's other BPP-cohort pages within the catalog. He is the organizational counterpart figure to Newton (the FamousNames Huey Newton page handles the latter), and the catalog's reading of those BPP-cohort transcripts attaches the founders' authority figures to the cosines those transcripts already show. He is not himself an interviewee in the Civil Rights History Project; his standalone catalog page is added so visitors searching his name find a landing-place that connects to the corpus's BPP-cohort discussion. His Wikipedia entry is not used as a citation; the catalog's source for biographical orientation is his own 1970 memoir.
biographical_paragraph: Seale was born October 22, 1936 in Liberty, Texas; his family moved to Oakland during the wartime defense-industry migration. He met Huey P. Newton at Merritt College in the early 1960s and the two co-founded the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense in Oakland in October 1966, with Seale taking the chairman's role and Newton the minister-of-defense title. He was one of eight defendants charged for the 1968 Democratic National Convention protests; after he repeatedly objected to Judge Julius Hoffman's denial of his Sixth Amendment right to counsel of choice, Hoffman ordered him bound and gagged in the courtroom and ultimately severed his case (leaving the Chicago Seven). Seale wrote Seize the Time (1970), the BPP's institutional memoir; subsequent works include A Lonely Rage (1978). The Newton-Seale partnership is the BPP organizational frame the corpus's seven BPP-identified interviewees worked within.

sources.title: Bobby Seale, Seize the Time: The Story of the Black Panther Party and Huey P. Newton
sources.publisher: Random House, 1970 (reprinted Black Classic Press, 1991); WorldCat OCLC 144696
